refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed absolute.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, congestion profiles) agreed with customer. Measure across multiple teleoperation sessions and load scenarios. Acceptance: sustained packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) over a minimum observation window (e.g., 1 hour continuous operation).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, retry logic, redundancy) based on detected network conditions to maintain packet loss within acceptable bounds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The NGHE solution shall define and document the agreed network conditions (bandwidth, latency, jitter, loss profile) under which the ≤1% packet loss acceptance criterion applies.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive packet prioritization and forward error correction (FEC) in the teleoperation communication stack to reduce effective packet loss seen by critical commands.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (dual network paths or multi-link aggregation) with automatic failover to mask single-link packet loss.
DP-C: Implement application-level acknowledgment and selective retransmission of critical teleoperation messages (commands, safety alerts) with timeout-based recovery.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (FEC/prioritization) + DP-C (selective retransmission) to provide layered resilience: network-level error correction plus application-level recovery for critical payloads.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a two-layer packet loss mitigation strategy: (1) Network layer: adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and priority queuing for teleoperation traffic; (2) Application layer: selective retransmission of critical commands and safety alerts with acknowledgment-based flow control and timeout recovery. This approach balances latency (FEC avoids full retransmission overhead), safety (critical messages guaranteed delivery), and adaptability (parameters tuned to measured network conditions).

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation communication logic (network + application layers) with the broader NGHE solution's command/feedback architecture. This is acceptable because teleoperation is a primary operational mode and packet loss is intrinsic to its design.
DP-D introduces dependency on real-time network condition monitoring (FR-NGHE-009.3), which couples to the Telemetry interface. This is a known, manageable coupling.
DP-D does not couple to vehicle dynamics, safety services logic, or site management—these remain independent.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is localized to the teleoperation subsystem and its communication stack. No evidence of axiomatic design violation (independence of FRs). Recommend explicit interface contracts between network layer (FEC/prioritization) and application layer (retransmission) to minimize hidden dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and well-characterized (e.g., typical latency, jitter, loss profile). Risk: If network conditions vary significantly or are not pre-agreed, the ≤1% target may be unachievable. Mitigation: Establish network SLA with customer before design finalization.

Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth, latency) is acceptable for teleoperation. Risk: Excessive FEC may increase latency beyond operator tolerance. Mitigation: Tune FEC parameters (code rate, block size) via simulation and field trials.

Assumption: Application-level retransmission does not introduce unacceptable command latency. Risk: Timeout-based recovery may delay critical safety commands. Mitigation: Set aggressive timeout thresholds and prioritize safety-critical messages.

Assumption: Telemetry interface can provide real-time packet loss metrics. Risk: If monitoring is delayed or inaccurate, adaptive tuning may be ineffective. Mitigation: Validate telemetry accuracy and latency in integration testing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC code rate and block size selection criteria; (2) Priority queue discipline (e.g., safety alerts > commands > telemetry); (3) Retransmission timeout thresholds and maximum retry counts; (4) Fallback behavior if packet loss exceeds threshold despite mitigation (e.g., operator alert, graceful degradation, emergency stop). These details will inform IM assignment and enable verification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including network-layer packet loss mitigation (FEC, priority queuing), application-layer message handling (selective retransmission, acknowledgment), and real-time packet loss monitoring. The module interfaces with the Vehicle (for command execution and telemetry feedback), Safety Services (for safety-critical message prioritization and alerts), and the Network Link (for physical communication).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from the tele-operator or site manager. Teleoperation module encodes these into prioritized packets with retransmission logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, packet loss metrics) back to the operator and monitoring systems. Teleoperation module monitors outbound telemetry packet loss and adapts FEC/retry parameters.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services (e.g., geofence violations, emergency stop triggers). Teleoperation module prioritizes these messages for guaranteed delivery. Also transmits packet loss degradation alerts to Safety Services if thresholds are exceeded.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical communication channel to the vehicle. Teleoperation module applies FEC encoding, priority queuing, and monitors packet loss on this link. Receives raw network statistics (loss, latency, jitter) for adaptive tuning.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, priority queuing, and selective retransmission are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces with Vehicle (command/feedback), Safety Services (alert prioritization), and Network Link (physical channel). High cohesion within the module.

Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics and control. Packet loss mitigation is not a vehicle-level concern; it is a communication concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would diffuse communication logic across vehicle control, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety logic (geofencing, emergency stop, hazard detection). While packet loss is a safety concern, the mitigation mechanism (FEC, retransmission) is communication-specific, not safety-specific. Safety Services should consume packet loss metrics and define safety-critical message priorities, but should not own the FEC/retransmission implementation. Partial containment acceptable for safety alert prioritization interface.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system integrator. Assigning DP-D here would diffuse communication logic across the entire system, preventing reuse and testability. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (two-layer packet loss mitigation). Rationale: (1) Teleoperation is the primary consumer of packet loss metrics and the primary actor in command/feedback exchange. (2) FEC and priority queuing are communication-layer concerns, not vehicle or safety concerns. (3) Selective retransmission is application-level logic specific to teleoperation message semantics. (4) Containment score of 95 reflects high cohesion and minimal diffusion. (5) Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and manageable.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if FEC implementation is not cleanly separated from retransmission logic. Mitigation: Decompose into sub-modules (FEC layer, retransmission layer, monitoring layer) in the next design level.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface requires Teleoperation to understand safety-critical message semantics (e.g., which messages must be retransmitted). Mitigation: Define a clear safety message taxonomy and priority scheme in the Safety Services module; Teleoperation consumes this as a configuration.

Risk: Network Link interface may expose low-level network statistics (loss, latency, jitter) that Teleoperation must interpret correctly. Mitigation: Establish a clear contract for Network Link metrics (sampling rate, accuracy, latency of reporting).

Risk: Adaptive tuning of FEC and retry parameters requires real-time feedback from Network Link. If feedback is delayed or inaccurate, adaptation may be ineffective. Mitigation: Validate Network Link monitoring in integration testing; set conservative fallback parameters.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to specify: (1) Sub-module decomposition: FEC/Priority Queue layer (network-level), Selective Retransmission layer (application-level), Packet Loss Monitor layer (metrics collection). (2) Interface contracts: Define exact format and semantics of Tasking (command encoding), Telemetry (feedback encoding), SafetyAlert (priority levels, guaranteed delivery semantics), and Network Link (loss/latency metrics). (3) Configuration interface: Teleoperation should consume FEC parameters, retry thresholds, and safety message priorities from external configuration (owned by Safety Services or system integrator), not hard-code them. (4) Ownership of Network Link: Clarify whether Network Link is owned by Teleoperation or is a shared infrastructure module; if shared, define clear SLA for packet loss metrics.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (two-layer packet loss mitigation) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (maintain ≤1% packet loss). The selected DP is necessary and sufficient: FEC reduces packet loss at the network layer, selective retransmission ensures critical messages are delivered at the application layer, and adaptive tuning allows the system to meet customer-defined thresholds under agreed network conditions. No independence violations detected; DP-D does not couple to unrelated FRs (vehicle dynamics, site management, etc.).

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module is a natural owner of DP-D. FEC, priority queuing, and retransmission are communication-specific concerns, not diffused across vehicle, safety, or system-level modules.

Moderate interface complexity: DP-D requires clear contracts with Vehicle (command/feedback encoding), Safety Services (alert prioritization), and Network Link (loss metrics). These interfaces are well-defined and manageable.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. The 5% diffusion is acceptable and localized to interface definitions (SafetyAlert priority semantics, Network Link metric contracts). Recommend explicit interface specifications in the next design level to minimize hidden dependencies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Packet loss is directly measurable via end-to-end testing under agreed network conditions. Verification approach: (1) Simulate or emulate network conditions (latency, jitter, loss) in a lab environment. (2) Run teleoperation sessions with varying network profiles. (3) Measure sustained packet loss over observation windows (e.g., 1 hour). (4) Verify that packet loss remains ≤1% (or customer-defined target) across all tested conditions. (5) Validate that FEC overhead and retransmission latency do not exceed operator tolerance. Feasibility is high because packet loss is a well-understood metric and Teleoperation module provides clear observability (Telemetry interface exports packet loss metrics).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. FR-NGHE-009 is allocated to the Teleoperation module with clear ownership. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) are allocated to Teleoperation subsystem design (next level). Acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss under agreed network conditions) are measurable and testable. No ambiguity in allocation or acceptance.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify FEC code rate, block size, and priority queue discipline. These details are necessary for IM implementation and verification.

Establish explicit interface contracts: (1) Tasking interface: command encoding, priority levels, timeout semantics. (2) Telemetry interface: feedback encoding, packet loss metric format, reporting frequency. (3) SafetyAlert interface: safety message taxonomy, priority levels, guaranteed delivery semantics. (4) Network Link interface: loss/latency metric format, sampling rate, accuracy requirements.

Define fallback behavior if packet loss exceeds threshold despite mitigation (e.g., operator alert, graceful degradation, emergency stop). This is a safety-critical detail that should be specified at this level.
Clarify ownership of Network Link module: Is it owned by Teleoperation, or is it a shared infrastructure module? If shared, define SLA for packet loss metrics (latency, accuracy, availability).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) that address distinct concerns: error recovery mechanisms, real-time monitoring, adaptive communication, and network condition documentation. Each sub-FR requires its own FR->DP->IM decomposition to ensure clarity, testability, and ownership. Additionally, DP-D (two-layer mitigation) is complex and benefits from sub-module decomposition (FEC layer, retransmission layer, monitoring layer) to manage implementation risk. Proceeding to the next level will refine interface contracts, clarify ownership boundaries, and enable detailed verification planning.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is well-formed and addresses a critical safety and quality-of-service concern. However, the sub-FRs (error recovery, monitoring, adaptation, network documentation) are distinct functional concerns that should be decomposed separately. DP-D is a sound solution strategy but requires detailed specification of FEC parameters, retry logic, and fallback behavior. The Teleoperation module is the correct owner, but its internal structure (FEC layer, retransmission layer, monitoring layer) should be designed in the next level. Proceeding to ZIGZAG_DOWN allows the design to mature without introducing unnecessary complexity at this level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, retry logic, redundancy) based on detected network conditions to maintain packet loss within acceptable bounds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The NGHE solution shall define and document the agreed network conditions (bandwidth, latency, jitter, loss profile) under which the ≤1% packet loss acceptance criterion applies.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and well-characterized before design finalization.
FEC overhead (bandwidth, latency) is acceptable for teleoperation use cases.
Application-level retransmission does not introduce unacceptable command latency.
Telemetry interface can provide real-time, accurate packet loss metrics.
Safety Services module will define safety-critical message priorities and guaranteed delivery semantics.
Network Link module will provide accurate loss/latency metrics with acceptable reporting latency.
Operator tolerance for command latency is ≥100ms (typical for heavy equipment teleoperation); FEC and retransmission must not exceed this budget.
open_risks: Network condition variability: If actual network conditions differ significantly from agreed conditions, packet loss may exceed ≤1% target. Mitigation: Establish network SLA with customer; conduct field trials under representative conditions.

FEC parameter tuning: Selecting optimal FEC code rate and block size requires simulation and field validation. Suboptimal tuning may result in excessive latency or insufficient error correction. Mitigation: Conduct sensitivity analysis; implement adaptive tuning based on measured network conditions.

Retransmission timeout selection: Aggressive timeouts may cause unnecessary retransmissions (wasting bandwidth); conservative timeouts may delay critical messages. Mitigation: Conduct latency analysis; set timeouts based on measured round-trip time plus safety margin.

Safety message prioritization: Teleoperation module must correctly interpret and prioritize safety-critical messages. Incorrect prioritization could compromise safety. Mitigation: Define clear safety message taxonomy in Safety Services; validate prioritization logic in integration testing.

Monitoring accuracy: If packet loss metrics are delayed or inaccurate, adaptive tuning may be ineffective. Mitigation: Validate Network Link monitoring in integration testing; set conservative fallback parameters.
evidence_needed: Network condition characterization: Measure typical latency, jitter, and packet loss profiles at representative worksites. Document agreed network conditions with customer.

FEC performance data: Conduct simulation or lab testing to characterize FEC overhead (latency, bandwidth) vs. error correction capability. Identify optimal code rates and block sizes for teleoperation.
Retransmission latency analysis: Measure round-trip time (RTT) under agreed network conditions. Determine acceptable timeout thresholds and maximum retry counts to meet operator latency tolerance.
Safety message taxonomy: Define safety-critical message types (emergency stop, geofence violation, sensor fault, etc.) and priority levels. Document in Safety Services module specification.

Integration test plan: Design tests to validate packet loss mitigation under simulated network conditions (latency, jitter, loss profiles). Measure sustained packet loss, command latency, and operator situational awareness.
Field trial plan: Conduct field trials at representative worksites to validate packet loss performance under real network conditions. Measure packet loss, operator feedback, and safety incidents.
